metadata.name: Fundamentals of Exercise, Nutrition, and Health
metadata.description: Core concepts in exercise physiology, nutrition science, anatomy, sports performance, and wellness.
metadata.author: Trivial World Team

questions.questionText: The heart is located in which area of the body?
questions.answerText: The chest (thoracic cavity), slightly left of center

questions.questionText: What is the name of the large muscle at the front of the upper arm that bends the elbow?
What organ located in the upper abdomen begins the chemical digestion of food?
What tough, fibrous tissue connects muscles to bones?
What flexible tissue covers the ends of bones at joints, preventing them from rubbing together?
What region of the spine is referred to as the "lumbar" region?
questions.answerText: The lower back (the five lumbar vertebrae, L1–L5)

questions.questionText: What organ, located behind the stomach, produces both digestive enzymes and the hormone insulin?
What is the name of the fibrous sac that surrounds and protects the heart?
What is the anatomical name for the collarbone?
What is the name of the large, flat triangular bone at the back of the shoulder, commonly called the shoulder blade?
What Olympic gymnast is widely considered the greatest of all time, having won the most World Championship medals in gymnastics history?
What sport did Michael Jordan play professionally?
What health trend involves eliminating gluten — a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye — from the diet?
What type of diet, popular in recent years, combines very high fat, moderate protein, and extremely low carbohydrates to push the body into a state called ketosis?
What tennis legend, who won 23 Grand Slam singles titles, retired in 2022?
What ancient practice originating in India combines physical postures, breathing exercises, and meditation?
questions.tidbits: The word "yoga" comes from a Sanskrit word meaning to unite or join.

questions.questionText: What former NFL quarterback created the "TB12 Method," a wellness and diet program he credits with extending his playing career into his mid-40s?
What fitness evaluation system developed by the Cooper Institute uses the PACER aerobic test and is commonly administered in U.S. schools?
What sprinter, known as "the fastest man alive," holds world records in both the 100m and 200m events?
What term, coined by researchers studying populations with the longest-living people on Earth, describes regions like Okinawa and Sardinia with high concentrations of centenarians?
What scientist discovered penicillin, the first antibiotic, in 1928?
What vitamin, found in citrus fruits, prevents the disease scurvy?
What English physician pioneered the smallpox vaccine in 1796?
questions.tidbits: Jenner noticed that milkmaids who contracted cowpox seemed immune to smallpox, leading to the first vaccine.

questions.questionText: What landmark U.S. law, passed in 1906, established the first federal oversight of food and drug safety?
questions.answerText: The Pure Food and Drug Act (which also led to the formation of the FDA)

questions.questionText: What scientist coined the term "vitamins" in 1912 to describe essential nutrients needed to prevent diseases like scurvy and beriberi?
What Polish-French scientist discovered two radioactive elements — polonium and radium — and was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize?
What ancient Greek physician, considered the father of medicine, emphasized observation and rational thinking in treating illness?
What 19th-century Hungarian physician discovered that hand-washing dramatically reduced deaths in hospitals, decades before germ theory was widely accepted?
What 1953 discovery by Watson and Crick, building on X-ray crystallography by Rosalind Franklin, revealed the double-helix structure of DNA?
questions.answerText: The double-helix structure of DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.tidbits: Watson and Crick's model relied heavily on unpublished X-ray images taken by Rosalind Franklin.

questions.questionText: What nutritional deficiency disease, once common in the southeastern United States, was caused by a lack of niacin (Vitamin B3) and was widespread where corn was the dietary staple?
What is the term for the practice of paying focused attention to the present moment without judgment, often used in stress reduction?
What chemical, often called the body's natural "feel-good" hormones, is released during exercise and helps reduce pain and boost mood?
What stage of sleep, characterized by rapid eye movement and vivid dreaming, is critical for emotional processing and memory consolidation?
What is the physiological "fight-or-flight" response, and what triggers it?
questions.answerText: A cascade of hormonal and physical changes — including increased heart rate, adrenaline release, and heightened alertness — triggered by a perceived threat or stressor

questions.questionText: What type of therapy, developed by Dr. Aaron Beck, helps patients identify and reframe negative thought patterns that contribute to depression and anxiety?
What Japanese philosophy, meaning "reason for being" or purpose in life, is associated with the longevity of people in Okinawa?
What evidence-based stress reduction program, developed by Jon Kabat-Zinn in 1979, combines mindfulness meditation with yoga?
What psychological model, developed by researcher Hans Selye, describes the three-stage bodily response to prolonged stress: alarm, resistance, and exhaustion?
What is cortisol, and what role does chronically elevated cortisol play in health?
questions.answerText: Cortisol is the primary stress hormone; chronically high levels can suppress the immune system, increase blood pressure, impair memory, and contribute to weight gain

questions.questionText: What term from positive psychology describes an optimal state of full engagement and enjoyment in an activity, where time seems to disappear?
questions.answerText: Flow (flow state), coined by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi

questions.questionText: What are the three macronutrients that provide the body with energy?
How many calories per gram does fat provide? A) 4, B) 7, C) 9, D) 12
questions.answerText: 9 (C) — compared to 4 calories per gram for carbohydrates and protein

questions.questionText: What vitamin does the skin produce when exposed to sunlight?
What is the Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)?
questions.answerText: The number of calories your body burns at rest to maintain basic functions like breathing, circulation, and cell repair

questions.questionText: What percentage of the human body is made up of water? A) 40%, B) 50%, C) 60%, D) 70%
questions.answerText: About 60% (C) — though this varies with age, sex, and body composition

questions.questionText: What role does dietary fiber play in digestion and overall health?
questions.answerText: Fiber adds bulk to stool, supports beneficial gut bacteria, and slows sugar absorption — reducing risk of constipation, heart disease, and blood sugar spikes

questions.questionText: What is the glycemic index, and why does it matter for energy and nutrition?
questions.answerText: The glycemic index (GI) measures how quickly a food raises blood glucose; low-GI foods provide steady energy, while high-GI foods cause rapid spikes and crashes

questions.questionText: What is the process called when the liver converts excess glucose into glycogen for storage?
What happens during EPOC (Excess Post-Exercise Oxygen Consumption) after intense exercise?
questions.answerText: The body continues to consume extra oxygen above resting levels to restore physiological systems — replenishing energy stores, clearing lactic acid, and repairing muscle — resulting in continued calo…

questions.questionText: What essential amino acid, which the body cannot synthesize on its own, is a precursor to serotonin and is found in high concentrations in eggs, turkey, and cheese?
What food group provides the primary fuel for endurance athletes during prolonged exercise?
questions.answerText: Carbohydrates (stored as glycogen in muscles and the liver)

questions.questionText: What does the acronym RICE stand for in sports injury first aid?
What is the purpose of warming up before intense exercise?
questions.answerText: To gradually increase heart rate and blood flow to muscles, raise core temperature, and prepare joints — reducing injury risk and improving performance

questions.questionText: What is the difference between a sprain and a strain in sports injuries?
questions.answerText: A sprain is a stretch or tear of a ligament (connecting bone to bone); a strain is a stretch or tear of a muscle or tendon

questions.questionText: What is "carbohydrate loading," and which type of athletes commonly use it?
questions.answerText: Increasing carbohydrate intake in the days before a long event to maximize muscle glycogen stores; used by marathon runners, cyclists, and triathletes

questions.questionText: What heart rate zone — expressed as a percentage of maximum heart rate — is generally considered the aerobic training zone? A) 40–50%, B) 50–60%, C) 60–80%, D) 80–100%
What is the role of electrolytes — including sodium, potassium, and magnesium — during exercise?
questions.answerText: Electrolytes regulate fluid balance, nerve signals, and muscle contractions; they are lost through sweat and must be replenished during prolonged exercise to prevent cramping and hyponatremia (low bl…

questions.questionText: What is periodization in athletic training?
questions.answerText: A systematic approach of cycling training volume and intensity through planned phases — base, build, peak, and recovery — to optimize performance and prevent overtraining

questions.questionText: What physiological adaptation in the cardiovascular system occurs after months of sustained aerobic training?
questions.answerText: The heart becomes stronger and more efficient (cardiac hypertrophy), pumping more blood per beat (increased stroke volume), resulting in a lower resting heart rate

questions.questionText: What is the concept of "progressive overload," and why is it the fundamental principle behind strength and conditioning gains?
questions.answerText: Progressive overload means gradually increasing the stress placed on the body during training; without this, the body adapts to its current workload and stops improving — making it the cornerstone of…
